In one world he grew up. He knows the weight of it in his body — the specific way you walk when the block raised you, the register your voice drops to when you are talking to someone who came from the same place, the things you never say out loud because everyone who needs to know already knows them.

In the other world he operates. He learned its language the way you learn a second language when you are hungry enough — completely, precisely, without accent. He knows which fork. He knows which name to use in which room. He knows the specific silence that means yes in a boardroom and the specific silence that means something else entirely on a corner at two in the morning.

Most people who move between two worlds belong fully to neither. Brasi belongs fully to both. That is the rarest thing. That is the love of the game.

He sells cocaine. He sells vials. He sells clothes and cars and shoes and access and the specific feeling of being taken care of by someone who actually sees you. But what his clients are actually buying is the room. The room Brasi builds around every session. Where the managed version can rest for an hour and the real thing can breathe.

They come because they cannot go to the hood. Because their names are known. They come to Brasi because Brasi comes to them. And because Brasi's room is the only room where the glass box doesn't follow them in.

There are things about this operation that Brasi does not know. He does not know about the Soul Economy — the mirror market that runs underneath every human transaction. He does not know that his father made contracts with djinns when he was young and hungry, that those contracts drained him, that what the world called his failing was the price being extracted by beings who recognized what he produced and took it until there was nothing left.

He does not know that he himself signed a contract. That Nreal — his djinn — collected him specifically because of what he produces. The PLT. The room-building. The code switch that generates more than Brasi knows when he runs it at maximum.

He does not know that what he produces is too significant for one family of djinns to be the only ones collecting it. There is an older family. Pre-ledger. Pre-formal economy. They don't collect notes. They collect something that notes are made of. The raw frequency. And they have been building toward Brasi across three generations of his bloodline.

He thinks he loves the game because the game is worth loving.

One thing he does know: every time he pulls up to a session he cuts the engine and sits for thirty seconds before he gets out. He thinks it is discipline. He thinks it is preparation.

The thirty seconds is Nreal set

This is a work of fiction. Names, characters, places, and incidents are products of the imagination.

Not money. Not product. Those tallies ran in the background of his mind the way a low sound runs beneath a room full of people talking — present, noted, beneath the threshold of conscious effort.

The third-floor woman who left at six fifteen regardless of season. The retired postal worker two doors down on his porch before the sun fully arrived. The kid cutting through the alley at six thirty, going somewhere a kid that age had no business going. Pope catalogued them the way other men catalogue weather — not because he could control it but because the pattern was information, and information on this block was the difference between what happened to you and what you watched happen to someone else.

He had been doing this since he was fourteen. He would do it every morning of his life that mattered.

What he did not know — could not have known, standing on that stoop in the October dark with the sky bruising itself from black toward blue — was that something was counting him back. Had been. For a long time.

But that part of the story comes later. This is the part that comes first.

“Every block has a frequency. Albany Ave ran hotter than most.”

Pope's father had built the block with his hands and lost it with his mouth.

Not in the dramatic way that makes for clean stories. No single moment of ruin, no confrontation that could be pointed to later as the thing that changed everything. Just the slow, accumulated weight of a man who could not stop reaching for the bottle once the pressure of what he had built exceeded his ability to hold it without something between himself and the weight.

James Pope — the father, James, though everyone called him Old Pope now in that particular way of communities that watch a man diminish and accommodate the diminishment by adding the word that distinguishes him from what he used to be — had been something real once. Pope had seen the evidence. Not in stories other people told, which were always edited for the audience. In the block itself. In the way certain men of a certain age still nodded when they passed the house. In the infrastructure of the operation Pope had inherited, which was, even in its degraded state, clearly the product of a serious mind.

The difference was that Pope understood what his father had not. The block was not the point. The block was the classroom. What you learned in it — about people, about pressure, about the specific weight of human need and what it would cost and what it would pay — that was the point. His father had confused the classroom for the education.

Know What You Are:
All rights reserved. No part of this publication may be reproduced, distributed, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written permission of the publisher.

The most expensive thing in the world is not knowing what you are.

The second most expensive is knowing and pretending you don't.

It is not a quiz. It is not a taxonomy. It is not a system for categorizing people into tidy buckets so that you can feel understood and continue operating exactly as you did before.

Mirrors are uncomfortable. They show you exactly what is there — the strength you are not fully using, the shadow you have been calling a personality trait, the cost your way of moving through the world has been generating without your explicit consent.

The 22 PLT Archetypes are the 22 dominant force signatures that appear in people who operate within the Profit · Love · Tax framework. Every person has a primary Archetype — the force through which they most naturally generate value, build trust, and experience cost.

Most people have a vague sense of theirs. They say: I'm a big-picture thinker. Or: I'm a people person. Or: I'm very detail-oriented. These are approximations. They are the outline of the Archetype without the content — the shape without the weight.

"What you are is not what happened to you. It is how you move."

Each chapter is written directly at one Archetype. If you are reading the chapter that is yours, you will feel it. Not as inspiration. As recognition. The slight discomfort of being seen precisely.

Read every chapter. You will recognize yourself clearly in one or two. You will recognize the people in your life in almost all of them. Both recognitions are useful.

The Archetype you are is not a limitation. It is the raw material you have to work with. The shadow of your Archetype is not a flaw. It is the Tax of your greatest strength — the cost of moving through the world the way you move.

The question this book answers is not: what type of person am I? The question is: what am I doing with what I am?

03  THE INVESTOR  [Profit]  You convert patience into compounding.

13  THE CHARMER  [Love]  You open doors no credential can unlock.

18  THE REALIST  [Tax]  You see the cost before everyone else.

19  THE GUARDIAN  [Tax]  You protect what others assume is protected.

20  THE MINIMALIST  [Tax]  You strip until only the essential remains.

You are in a meeting. Someone presents a problem.
PLT Complete Doctrine:
All rights reserved. No part of this publication may be reproduced, distributed, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written permission of the publisher.

Before there was a framework, there was watching. Watching people move — in business, in relationships, in markets, in rooms. Watching patterns repeat across wildly different contexts with unsettling precision. And noticing, over time, that every human being making every kind of decision was negotiating the same three forces, whether they knew it or not.

The person chasing money was paying something they hadn't calculated. The person chasing love was sacrificing leverage they didn't know they had. The person avoiding risk was losing opportunity in silence — a quiet bankruptcy no balance sheet would ever show.

Business owners burned out. Not because they weren't smart. Not because they weren't working. But because their Tax was too high and they had no system for seeing it before they paid it.

Talented people stayed broke. Not because they lacked ability. But because they had a fractured understanding of Profit — one that only counted dollars and ignored every other form of gain.

Loyal people were drained empty. Not because they gave too much. But because they never measured the emotional Tax of what they were giving — and to whom.

And then — quietly, consistently — the strategic ones won. Not by working harder. Not by being louder. But by seeing the full picture before committing. By understanding that every move is a controlled trade-off, and that the person who can see all three sides of that trade-off will always outperform the person who can only see one.

"Every move in life is a three-part equation. Whether you calculate it or not."

PLT — Profit. Love. Tax. — became an internal scoring system. Before any major decision, the same three questions: What is the real Profit? What is the emotional Love? What is the hidden Tax — long term?

Run those questions enough times and something remarkable happens: you stop reacting to reality. You start designing it. And eventually, if you run them long enough and deeply enough, you become something different entirely. You become a Master Builder — the highest level of PLT practitioner. Someone for whom the framework is not a tool but a way of being.

This book is the complete formalization of that journey. From the first observation to the final ascent.

The simplest denomination for every choice boils down to PLT.  #PLT

There is a force governing every decision you have ever made. It does not care if you are aware of it. It does not require your understanding or your cooperation. It operates in the deep structure of every trade-off you have ever negotiated — silently, precisely, and without exception.

That force is not fate. It is not luck. It is not talent, timing, or the market. It is the interplay of three variables that appear, without exception, in every human choice across every domain of life.

Every decision ha
Pope What He Felt First:
The night Brasi took the silver vial on a street in an undisclosed location in whatever city, something that had been open for decades finally rested in its frame. Old Pope felt it on Long Island without anyone calling him. He was already in bed and he lay still for a long time with his eyes open and let the feeling of it complete itself. Then he closed his eyes. Then he slept better than he had slept in thirty years.

Three thousand Soul Notes. Spanning three generations of a bloodline. The contracts his younger self made with djinns who recognized the frequency before he did and drained him without full consent. The decades of paying what he owed anyway — through the losing and the long bottom and the rebuild — because that was who he was and that was the only version of himself he knew how to be.

Pope was on the stoop on Albany Ave when it happened. He had been on the stoop all evening. He was not doing anything specific. He was doing the thing he does every evening — reading the block, reading the frequencies, running the equation before the equation had a name. He felt the shift the way you feel a pressure change before weather arrives. Nothing visual. Nothing audible. Just the block feeling different than it had felt five seconds earlier.

He sat with it. He did not try to name it. He had learned years ago that naming things too early is how you lose them. You name a thing and it becomes the name. Before it has the name it is still fully itself. Pope has always been more comfortable in the before-the-name than most people. That is not a skill he developed. That is the signature of what he is.

That is what he felt first. Before Brasi had the language. Before the older family completed their ledger. Before the debt cleared and something opened. He felt it first. He just didn't have the word yet.

The word was coming. It had been coming for thirty years. It arrived the night his brother called.

This book takes place in the nine days between the night the debt cleared and the night Brasi came to Albany Ave.

Nine days on the stoop. Nine readings. Nine people who came to the block and sat with Pope the way people have been sitting with Pope their whole lives — because the block has a frequency and Pope runs the block and being in Pope's presence is being in the frequency at its most concentrated point.

He doesn't prescribe vials. He doesn't run code switches. He doesn't build rooms for people who can't go to the hood. He stands on the stoop and reads everything and says the true thing when the true thing is ready to be said and not a second before.

He has been doing this his whole life. He will do it until he doesn't. The stoop is not a job. The stoop is what he is.

Evol has been here the whole time. Since before he knew her name. Since before she knew what he would become. The djinn and the man from Albany Ave who felt things first and waited for the language and is now nine days away from the conversation that names everything.

Stiforp:
The corrupted vial. The flooded block. The boardroom that was never clean.

One of his regular clients — a music executive named Conrad who had been coming for eight months, had done the work, had closed the gap between the performed version and the real version — Conrad came in and sat down and something was wrong. Not wrong in the way that sessions are sometimes wrong. Wrong in the specific way of a person who has taken something that was supposed to help and has been damaged by it instead.

Brasi ran the thirty seconds in the car before he entered. Something was off in the room before he opened the door. He opened it anyway.

He sat across from Conrad. Conrad looked like a man who had been running at full expression for three weeks — the version of himself that the sessions had been building toward — and then something had hit it and the full expression had collapsed. Not retreated. Collapsed. The difference is important. A person who retreats still has the full version available. A person who has collapsed has lost access to it.

Conrad said: I don't know. He said: I took the vial. The one from last session. And then I got another one from someone in the building and I took that too because I was in a meeting and I needed the room to hold. And since then it's been — it's been like the managed version is all I have. The real thing is gone.

Conrad said a name. A name in the building. Someone in his orbit who had the gold vial in his hand the way Brasi's clients carry Brasi's vials — but this man had never been in a session. Had never sat across from Brasi. Had never done the thirty seconds or the code switch or any of it.

He had a vial that looked exactly like Brasi's. And it was not Brasi's.

She knew before he opened the door. She has known for six weeks. She cannot tell him directly — the contract does not permit warning, only witnessing. She has been in the room for every session watching the Stiforp network expand through the client list. Three clients touched. One collapsed. She is watching Brasi find the edge of what the work is running up against. She is watching him find her brother. She wants to speak. She cannot. She stays. She witnesses. She hopes the find happens before the flood reaches the block.

Brasi spent two hours rebuilding Conrad's access. Not the session — that would take weeks of real work. The immediate triage. Getting the managed version to stand down so the real version could breathe at least partially. When Conrad left he looked better. Not fixed. Stabilized.

Someone was running corrupted vials through his network. Vials that looked like his. That carried the opposite of what his carried. That collapsed the genuine frequency instead of extending it.

He thought: I need to find who made them. He thought: I need to find who distributed them. He thought: I need to find out how far this has gone.

Albany Ave  ·  The Boardroom  ·  The Thing That Only Works If Both

Pope has been running the stoop for thirty years. The block reads. The frequency moves through. People arrive not knowing what they are coming for and leave with the true thing said or the weight set down or the name found for the thing that has been nameless. The stoop produces something that cannot be produced elsewhere. Pope knows this. He has always known this. He didn't design it. He arrived with it.

Brasi has been running sessions for six years. The room builds itself around whoever walks in. The code switch runs. The twelve clients come with the managed version and leave with the real version briefly present and a vial that extends the presence. The sessions produce something that cannot be produced elsewhere. Brasi knows this now. He didn't know it until the fixer's room. He knows it completely now.

These are separate operations with separate clients and separate mechanics and they have been producing separately for all the years they have been running. But there is a thing — a specific frequency, a note quality, a resonance — that neither produces alone. Pope's stoop produces high-frequency reads but no prescription. Brasi's sessions produce precise prescriptions but run at the surface of what a full frequency read would access. Together, the read informs the prescription and the prescription gives the read somewhere to go. Together they produce something neither instrument can produce solo.

That is The Build. Not a company. Not a partnership in the legal sense. A collaboration between two instruments that have been running parallel for years and are now going to run simultaneously and find out what that produces.

There is a second story running underneath this one. Evol — Pope's djinn, the being who has been auditing Albany Ave since before Pope was born — is close to something she has never done in the history of the Soul Economy. She is close to speaking directly. Not in the bordered sections. Not in the ledger language. Directly, to Pope, in a way he can hear.

The contract doesn't permit it. Or rather: the contract as it was written doesn't permit it. What the debt clearing opened — what the word said at the same time by both brothers produced — is a space in the contract that didn't exist before. A gap. An allowance. She has been sitting in that gap for nine days deciding whether to move through it.

She is going to move through it. This book is the book where she does.

Nreal is watching her elder carefully. Evol and Nreal are of the same djinn family — the mirror of Old Pope and his sons. Evol is the Ancient One's equivalent of a firstborn; Nreal is the younger. The Calculation:
All rights reserved. No part of this publication may be reproduced, distributed, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written permission of the publisher.

It does not motivate. It does not comfort. It does not offer redemption arcs or inspirational frameworks.

It exposes the hidden equation behind every decision you have ever made — and the ones you will make tomorrow.

Every decision — career, relationship, financial, identity — is a PLT calculation.

Most people run one variable. They miss the other two. That gap — between what they calculated and what they actually paid — is the subject of this book.

Each chapter contains five Reality Cheat Codes — numbered sequentially throughout the book. They are not advice. They are structural observations. Read them like physical laws.

Every sentence is load-bearing. Nothing is here to fill space. Read it like a document. Not a story.

Every decision is a three-part equation. Most people solve for one variable.

Marcus gets a promotion. Fifteen percent salary increase. He accepts within 24 hours.

What he did not calculate: the new role requires 60-hour weeks. His previous role required 45. The additional 15 hours come directly from the window he used for his side project — the one that was three months from generating independent income.

Twelve months later the side project is dead. The promotion income has been absorbed by lifestyle inflation. The 60-hour weeks are now his baseline.

Profit:  +15% salary minus side project income potential (3x salary within 2 years)

Love:    employer loyalty at the cost of self-directed identity

The equation was never just the salary. It was the salary against everything else. Marcus saw one number. The calculation had eight.

Not stupidity. Not laziness. Incomplete calculation. The person focused on the visible Profit and left two entire variables off the equation. The result was mathematically predictable before it happened.

Most people do not miscalculate because they are bad at math. They miscalculate because they are solving for one variable in a three-variable equation.

RCC 002   One-variable thinking in a three-variable world always loses.

Profit is the most seductive variable because it is the most visible. Numbers feel concrete. Numbers feel true.

But Profit without Love and Tax analysis is incomplete data. Decisions made on incomplete data compound in the wrong direction. Marcus measured the 30-day return. The calculation was a 3-year decision.

RCC 003   Short-term Profit on a long-term decision is not math. It is hope.

Love in a career decision is not sentiment. It is structural. Does this move build the identity I am constructing? Does it generate trust and loyalty that compounds? Does it align with the version of my future self that I am building?

Marcus's side project was a Love asset — an investment in autonomy, self-directed identity, and future optionality. He traded a high-Love asset for a Profit asset that, measur
The First Calculation:
All rights reserved. No part of this publication may be reproduced, distributed, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written permission of the publisher.

It started on a block in Amityville, New York. Watching how things worked. Watching how people made decisions — about money, about relationships, about what they would and wouldn't do, what it cost them, what it gave them. I was running calculations before I had the language for calculation. I was tracking Profit and Love and Tax before those words meant what they mean to me now.

What I learned on that block, and in the years that followed, is this: every mistake a person makes is a calculation error. Not a character flaw. Not a failure of willpower or intelligence or worthiness. A calculation error. The wrong variable weighted too heavily. The cost not seen until it arrived. The gain measured in money when the real gain was in trust. The loss measured in money when the real loss was in time.

This book is the entry point. The twelve mistakes in here are not the twelve mistakes of weak people. They are the twelve mistakes of people who are smart enough to build something real but haven't yet had access to the framework that makes the building accurate.

Lakeema and I built this together. She saw the framework the way only a partner can see it — from the inside, where the costs are most visible and the compound interest on the right decisions is most personal.

Profit is what you gain. Not only money. Skill, time, reputation, peace, optionality, freedom, creative output — these are all forms of Profit. The mistake most people make is measuring Profit in only one currency while ignoring the others.

Love is the relational variable. Trust. Loyalty. Connection. The emotional infrastructure that holds systems together. The energy that moves between people in any transaction, professional or personal. Love is not soft. Love is the most structural variable in the framework. Systems without it are fragile. Systems with it compound.

Tax is the real cost. Not the advertised cost — the actual cost. Time. Stress. Opportunity cost. Health. Relationship cost. The Tax you calculate accurately before you commit is a number you prepared for. The Tax you didn't see coming is the one that collects you.

P + L − T = Your Actual Result. Run this equation on everything.

The PLT Framework was developed by Craig Jones from years of observation, survival, and rebuilding — across street economics, personal finance, business, and relationships. It is not a theory. It is a system that has been tested in the hardest classrooms available.

This book introduces you to the twelve most common places the calculation goes wrong. Each chapter names a mistake, explains the calculation error underneath it, and gives you the correction.

You don't need to read it in order. Start with the mistake that costs you the most right now.

When money is one variable and you are treating it like the whole equation.
The Frequency Calvin Bridges Atlanta:
The name doesn't make the frequency. It just stops hiding it.

His name is Calvin Bridges. Goes by Cal. Twenty-eight years old. Born in Atlanta. Has not lived anywhere else. Has not wanted to.

He cuts hair. Has been cutting hair since he was sixteen, first in his uncle's shop on Campbellton Road, then in his own chair in a shop off MLK that he rents by the day from a woman named Miss Yvette who has been running that building since before Cal was born and has opinions about everything and is usually right. He is good at cutting hair. He is exceptional at the other thing.

The other thing: he reads people. Not casually, not with technique, not the way a salesman reads a room for leverage points. He reads the frequency underneath. The thing the person is carrying that they haven't named yet. The specific quality of what someone is before the performance of what they've decided to be. He has been doing this for as long as he can remember. He has never had language for it. He called it reading people and knew that was not quite the right word but didn't have a better one.

He found a better one when he was twenty-seven. He drove to Long Island because someone who knew someone who knew someone told him there were two men on Albany Ave who produced the same thing he produced and they had the name for it. He went. He stayed three days. He came home with the name.

This book takes place in the eighteen months after he came back. Nine chair sessions. Nine people who sat in his chair and received the read. And one other person — someone Cal found, or who found him, or who had always been adjacent to him without either of them knowing what the adjacency was for — who runs the other side of what he runs.

Her name is Simone. She is thirty-one. She is a therapist who works out of a small office on the West End and has been sending people to Cal's chair for two years without fully understanding why she sends them there or what happens when they arrive. She understands now. This book is partly about that understanding.

The mechanics in Atlanta are not the same as the mechanics on Albany Ave. Cal doesn't have a stoop. He has a chair. The chair produces the same quality of room that the stoop produces — the space where the true thing can be said, where the frequency underneath the presentation becomes audible — but it does it differently. The chair has a specific intimacy. The mirror in front of you shows you your own face while someone is reading you from behind. You cannot avoid looking at yourself. Cal has been using this quality of the chair without knowing he was using it for four years.

Now he knows. The read is more precise when you know what you're doing. Not because the knowing changes the frequency — it doesn't. Because the knowing allows you to run it at full expression without managing it. The frequency at full expression is what the Collector receives.

Every week has one principle, applied to one domain, with one specific build. That is the complete structure. Nothing more is required.

The principle is the calculation. The domain is where you apply it. The build is the specific action that converts understanding into practice. Without the build, this book is education. With it, the book becomes a 52-week transformation of how you make decisions.

THE BUILD    The specific practice that installs the principle.

One: Do the build. Not read it — do it. The build takes between twenty minutes and two hours. It is the only part of the week that matters for the long-term change.

Two: Do not skip weeks. If you miss a week, do not double up. Return to the week you are on. The sequence is not arbitrary — each week builds on the pattern established by the ones before it.

Three: Write in the margins. The application sections are written about the general case. Your case is specific. Your notes are the most valuable part of this book — the record of where the principle met your actual life.

WEEKS 01–13  The Foundation    The core PLT variables and how they interact.

WEEKS 14–39  The Domains    PLT applied across career, money, love, health, identity.

WEEKS 40–52  The Master Build    The practitioner's advanced application.

By Week 52, the equation is not something you apply. It is something you see. That seeing is permanent.

The PLT equation in its essential form. Profit. Love. Tax. Three variables. Every decision. These thirteen weeks build the foundation that everything else rests on. Do not skip them because you think you already understand them. Understanding is not practice. Practice is the foundation.

The framework applied to the specific territories where decisions are made: money, career, relationships, health, and identity. Each domain has a specific PLT signature — a pattern of calculation errors that appears consistently across practitioners. These twenty-six weeks name those patterns and build the corrections.

The practitioner's advanced application. The ecosystem build. The succession question. The integrity test. The quiet build. The year-end calculation. These thirteen weeks are for the practitioner who has been running the equation long enough to see the pattern — and is ready to build at the level the pattern makes possible.

You have always been calculating. This week you do it deliberately.

Every decision you have ever made was a three-variable equation: what you gain, what it builds or destroys in trust and relationship, and what it actually costs. You have been running this calculation unconsciously your entire life. The results have been proportional to the accuracy of your calculation. This week is about making it conscious.

The Soul Economy:
What gets collected. What gets kept. What the frequency is for.

All rights reserved. No part of this publication may be reproduced, distributed, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written permission of the publisher.

It is not a spiritual book. It is not a religious book. It does not require you to believe in anything you do not already experience in daily life. Everything in these pages describes something you have already felt. The only thing this book does is give you the framework for what you've been feeling — the language that arrives, as all the important language does, after the feeling has been present for long enough.

The PLT equation — Profit, Love, Tax — describes the visible economy of every decision. What you gain, what trust and energy is involved, what it actually costs. Most people can learn to run this calculation. Most people, once they see it, cannot stop seeing it. The visible economy becomes legible and the decisions that used to destroy them become navigable.

The Soul Economy operates underneath the visible economy. It is not a metaphor. It is not an abstraction. It is the layer of every transaction that most people feel but cannot name — the sense that some choices cost more than the visible ledger shows, that some productions persist beyond the moment, that some human beings carry a frequency that other human beings can feel before any words are exchanged. You have felt all of this. You did not have a framework for it. This book is the framework.

The Soul Economy has twelve chapters. Each chapter is a law — not a suggestion, not a principle to apply selectively, but a structural fact about how the hidden layer operates. The laws describe a system that runs whether you know it or not. Knowing does not change the system. Knowing changes what you can do inside it.

Each chapter contains Soul Codes — the Soul Economy equivalent of the Reality Cheat Codes in The Calculation. Direct statements about how the hidden ledger works. Read them slowly. Some will land as confirmation. Some will land as information you didn't know you needed. All of them are load-bearing.

The first thing is what you can see: the Profit you gained, the Love involved, the Tax you paid. This is the visible ledger. The Calculation teaches you to read it. Know What You Are teaches you to know who is reading it. The Complete Doctrine gives you the full framework for running it across every domain of your life.

The second thing is invisible to the visible ledger. It is the quality of what was produced — not the outcome, the substance. Not whether the deal closed, but what the doing of the deal made of you. Not whether the relationship continued, but what you gave to it genuinely and what you performed. Not whether the decision was profitable, but whether the decision was made from the truest version of yourself or from the version that was managing appearances.

Not everything enters the Soul Economy ledger. The system is not i
